svg.aria-label: Devon's whiteboard rendered as four cells comparing REST (the actual), SOAP, GraphQL, and gRPC. Each cell shows the shape's request envelope. Hover or tap a cell for the counterfactual Asha email.
svg.title: Four API shapes: REST as the actual; SOAP, GraphQL, gRPC as counterfactuals
svg.desc: Devon's whiteboard sketch comparing four API shapes. Top left: REST with a copper badge reading the actual, showing the request line GET /v1/books?q=mystery. Top right: SOAP with a stylized XML envelope wrapping a Search for mystery. Bottom left: GraphQL with POST /graphql and a query for books. Bottom right: gRPC with SearchBooks called for mystery as a binary call over HTTP/2. The REST cell is emphasized with a 2px copper border; the other three cells have 1px sage borders. Hovering or tapping a cell reveals the counterfactual Asha email for that shape and what Greenfield's reply would owe back.

g.aria-label: REST cell, the actual shape Greenfield ships
g.data-tooltip: Asha emailed Devon asking for POST /search. Devon politely declined. The verb stays GET, the URL stays /v1/books?q=mystery. The cache holds it. Atlas calls it twenty times an hour.

Asha would have asked: "send us the WSDL." Marginalia would have generated a client from the WSDL. Every Atlas call to find a book would have wrapped the query in an XML envelope. The cache would have stayed out of it.

Asha would have asked for the schema. Atlas would have written a query asking for exactly the fields it needed. One URL for every call. The cache would have stayed out of it because every call is a POST.

Asha would have asked for the proto file. Marginalia would have built the generated Atlas client from it. The call would have gone binary over HTTP/2. Any browser without grpc-web could not have called it. Any proxy not speaking HTTP/2 would have dropped the connection.
